text: First published: 04 October 2017 Since its inception about two decades ago, GROMACS has developed into one of the most popular simulation software packages for biomolecular simulations. Starting from an era where a single core was the common unit of computer processing power to nowadays where the world’s fastest supercomputers consist of hundreds of thousands of such units is a formidable development. In this article we describe the many architectural and algorithmic changes that needed to be implemented for a simulation program to be capable of making efficient use of these resources. This involves turning GROMACS from a single-core performance optimised program into a multi-level parallel program that in principle fully utilises resources provided by wide SIMD operations, GPU acceleration and hybrid (MPI/OpenMP) multi-core processing, for which we also had to replace the present molecular dynamics integrator with a cluster algorithm that allows neighbours of particles to be located both in the processor and in accelerated memory. In addition we had to distribute tasks to fully remove intra-node strong scaling performance barriers, to implement schemes to handle slowly evolving long-range components, and to use techniques from ensemble simulation to fully exploit the massive resources now available. We highlight all these developments in the simulations of a 1.2 μm long single-c strand DNA polymer chain. We also give an overview of the changes in our development process that were also required to manage our open-source code to obtain a high performance simulation program such as that described in the rest of this paper. These include moving GROMACS to C++, implementation of rigorous code reviewing in the coding workflow and the implementation of full automatic testing of all parts of the codebase.
explanation: The passage is coherent, domain-specific, and resembles a scholarly abstract about GROMACS with plausible technical details. No obvious AI-generated artefacts stand out.
